quiz_questions.question_text: What is the range of {3, 7, 10, 15, 20}?
quiz_questions.explanation: The range is the difference between the maximum and minimum values: 20 - 3 = 17.

quiz_questions.question_text: Find the interquartile range (IQR) if Q1 = 10 and Q3 = 25.
quiz_questions.explanation: IQR = Q3 - Q1 = 25 - 10 = 15. The IQR measures the spread of the middle 50% of the data.

quiz_questions.question_text: For the data set {2, 4, 6, 8, 10}, what is Q1?
quiz_questions.explanation: Q1 is the median of the lower half {2, 4}. The median of these two values is (2 + 4) / 2 = 3.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which measure of spread is resistant to outliers?

options.text: Standard deviation
Range
IQR
Variance

quiz_questions.explanation: The IQR only measures the spread of the middle 50% of the data, so outliers in the tails do not affect it.

quiz_questions.question_text: A data set has a standard deviation of 0. This means:

options.text: The data has outliers
All values are the same
The mean is 0
The data has one value

quiz_questions.explanation: A standard deviation of 0 means there is no variation in the data, so every value in the data set is identical.

quiz_questions.question_text: The range of {5, 12, 18, 25, 40} is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Range = maximum - minimum = 40 - 5 = 35.

quiz_questions.question_text: If every value in a data set is increased by 10, the standard deviation:

options.text: Decreases by 10
Increases by 10
Doubles
Stays the same

quiz_questions.explanation: Adding a constant to every value shifts the data but does not change the distances between values, so the standard deviation remains the same.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which data set has the greater spread: Set A {10, 10, 10, 10} or Set B {5, 10, 15, 20}?

options.text: Set B
Cannot be determined
Set A
They are equal

quiz_questions.explanation: Set A has a range of 0 (all values are identical), while Set B has a range of 15. Set B clearly has greater spread.

quiz_questions.question_text: The five-number summary consists of:

options.text: Mean, median, mode, range, IQR
Mean, variance, standard deviation, min, max
Min, Q1, median, Q3, max

quiz_questions.explanation: The five-number summary is composed of the minimum, first quartile (Q1), median (Q2), third quartile (Q3), and maximum.

quiz_questions.question_text: If every value in a data set is multiplied by 3, the standard deviation:

options.text: Is multiplied by 3
Stays the same
Is multiplied by 9
Is divided by 3

quiz_questions.explanation: Multiplying every value by a constant c multiplies the standard deviation by |c|. So multiplying by 3 triples the standard deviation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the range of a data set?

options.text: The most common value
The difference between the maximum and minimum values
The middle value of the data set
The average of all values

quiz_questions.explanation: The range is the simplest measure of spread, calculated by subtracting the smallest value from the largest value in the data set.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the interquartile range (IQR)?

options.text: The difference between the third quartile (Q3) and the first quartile (Q1), representing the spread of the middle 50% of data
The range of the entire data set, particularly regarding middle following conventional understanding
The average of Q1 and Q3, particularly regarding making in accordance with classical theory
The standard deviation squared, particularly regarding middle when measured under controlled conditions

quiz_questions.explanation: The IQR is Q3 minus Q1 and measures how spread out the middle half of the data is, making it resistant to outliers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is standard deviation?

options.text: The difference between the highest and lowest values
A measure of how spread out data values are from the mean
The middle value of a sorted data set
The sum of all data values

quiz_questions.explanation: Standard deviation measures the typical distance between each data point and the mean. A larger standard deviation means more spread out data.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is variance?

options.text: The square root of the standard deviation
The average of the squared differences from the mean
The difference between Q3 and Q1
The range divided by 2

quiz_questions.explanation: Variance is the average of the squared deviations from the mean. The standard deviation is the square root of the variance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a quartile?

options.text: The average of the data set
A type of graph
A value that divides a data set into four equal parts
A value that divides a data set into two equal parts

quiz_questions.explanation: Quartiles are values that divide ordered data into four equal groups. Q1, Q2 (the median), and Q3 mark the boundaries of each quarter.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'measure of spread' mean?

options.text: A type of graph showing data
A value that tells you the center of the data
A statistic that describes how much variability or dispersion exists in a data set
The most common data value

quiz_questions.explanation: Measures of spread describe how much the data values vary from one another and from the center. Examples include range, IQR, variance, and standard deviation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the five-number summary?

options.text: Mean, median, mode, range, and standard deviation
The first five values in a data set
Five different measures of center
A set of five descriptive statistics -- minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum -- that summarize a data set

quiz_questions.explanation: The five-number summary provides a quick snapshot of a data set's distribution using the minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum values.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'deviation' mean in statistics?

options.text: The range of the data set
The average of all values
The distance between a data value and the mean of the data set
The most frequently occurring value

quiz_questions.explanation: A deviation is the difference between an individual data point and the mean. Positive deviations are above the mean and negative deviations are below it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'spread' of a data set?

options.text: How far apart the data values are from each other and from the center
The shape of the distribution
The center of the data
The number of data points

quiz_questions.explanation: Spread describes the variability or dispersion of data values. Greater spread means values are more widely distributed, while less spread means they are closer together.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'dispersion' mean in a data set?

options.text: The median of the data
The degree to which data values are scattered or spread out from the center
The shape of a histogram
The number of values in the data set

quiz_questions.explanation: Dispersion is another word for spread. It measures how much the data values differ from one another, often quantified by range, IQR, or standard deviation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A farmer measures the heights (in cm) of 7 tomato plants: 30, 32, 35, 33, 31, 34, 36. What is the range of the plant heights?
quiz_questions.explanation: The range is the maximum minus the minimum: 36 - 30 = 6 cm. This tells the farmer that plant heights vary by 6 cm.

quiz_questions.question_text: A quality inspector records the weights (in grams) of 8 cereal boxes: 498, 502, 500, 497, 503, 499, 501, 500. Another factory produces boxes with weights: 480, 520, 490, 510, 495, 505, 488, 512. Which factory has more consistent production?

options.text: The second factory, because it has a wider range
Both factories are equally consistent
Cannot be determined without the mean
The first factory, because its data has less spread (smaller range and standard deviation)

quiz_questions.explanation: The first factory's range is 503 - 497 = 6g, while the second is 520 - 480 = 40g. The much smaller range of the first factory indicates more consistent production.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher gives a test and the scores are {55, 60, 65, 70, 72, 75, 78, 80, 85, 90, 92, 95}. What is the IQR of the test scores?
Two students track their daily screen time (in hours) over 5 days. Student A: {2, 2, 3, 2, 3}. Student B: {1, 5, 2, 4, 3}. Which student's screen time is more consistent?

options.text: Student B, because the values are more varied
Both are equally consistent
Student A, because the data has less variability (smaller standard deviation)
Cannot be determined

quiz_questions.explanation: Student A's range is 1 hour (3-2) while Student B's range is 4 hours (5-1). Student A's values are much closer together, showing more consistency.

quiz_questions.question_text: A delivery company tracks package weights (in lbs): 2, 3, 5, 8, 10, 12, 15, 50. An analyst uses the IQR instead of the range to describe spread. Why is the IQR a better choice here?

options.text: The IQR is always larger than the range
The IQR uses the mean instead of the median
The range is too difficult to calculate
The IQR is not affected by the 50-lb outlier, giving a better picture of typical spread

quiz_questions.explanation: The 50-lb package is an outlier that inflates the range to 48. The IQR focuses on the middle 50% of the data, providing a spread measure that is not distorted by extremes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coach records 100-meter dash times (in seconds) for a sprinter over 6 races: 11.2, 11.5, 11.3, 11.4, 11.1, 11.5. What is the range of the sprinter's times?
quiz_questions.explanation: Range = max - min = 11.5 - 11.1 = 0.4 seconds. This small range indicates the sprinter is very consistent.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical researcher records blood pressure readings for 10 patients on two different medications. Medication A has a standard deviation of 5 mmHg, and Medication B has a standard deviation of 15 mmHg. What can the researcher conclude?

options.text: Medication B is more effective
Medication A produces more consistent (less variable) blood pressure results among patients
Both medications produce equally consistent results
Medication A is more effective

quiz_questions.explanation: A smaller standard deviation means less variability. Medication A's patients have readings more tightly clustered around the mean, indicating more predictable outcomes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student records daily temperatures (in degrees F) for a week: 68, 70, 72, 71, 69, 73, 70. The student then adds 5 degrees to each reading to correct a calibration error. How does this change the standard deviation?

options.text: It increases by 5
It decreases by 5
It stays exactly the same
It doubles

quiz_questions.explanation: Adding a constant to every value shifts the entire data set without changing the distances between values, so the standard deviation remains unchanged.

quiz_questions.question_text: A shoe company measures foot lengths (in cm) for a new product. Sample A: {24, 25, 25, 26, 26, 27}. Sample B: {20, 22, 25, 26, 29, 30}. Which sample should the company use to design a shoe that fits most people?

options.text: Neither sample is useful, particularly regarding product when considering all
Both samples are equally useful when evaluated systematically
Sample B, because it has a larger range following the established framework
Sample A, because its smaller spread means a single shoe size will fit more people in that group

quiz_questions.explanation: Sample A has a range of 3 cm with tightly clustered values, meaning a single shoe size will accommodate more people. Sample B's range of 10 cm suggests more sizes would be needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: An investment analyst compares two stocks over the past year. Stock X had a mean return of 8% with a standard deviation of 2%, while Stock Y had a mean return of 8% with a standard deviation of 10%. Which stock is the safer investment?

options.text: Stock Y, because higher standard deviation means higher reward
Stock X, because its lower standard deviation means returns are more predictable and less volatile
Both are equally safe since the mean returns are the same
Cannot be determined from this information

quiz_questions.explanation: Both stocks have the same mean return, but Stock X's smaller standard deviation (2% vs 10%) means its returns are far more predictable and less risky.
